title: Logic and Modelling
description: Logic and Modelling. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP21111, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 2, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered By the Department of Computer Science. Available as a Free Choice Unit? Yes. Overview & Syllabus: This course focuses heavily on logic, reasoning algorithms, and formal methods. It explores several types of logic that are widely utilized in practical applications, ranging from mathematical reasoning to the verification of both software and hardware. Core topics include the formal aspects of propositional logic, quantified propositional logic (QBF), and linear temporal logic (LTL). Additionally, the module covers major reasoning algorithms associated with these logics, such as DPLL, tableaux, randomized algorithms, and Ordered Binary Decision Diagrams (OBDDs). Finally, the curriculum delves into the formalization of complex problems, teaching students how to specify state transition systems and verify their properties using logical frameworks. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP11120 (Mathematical Techniques for Computer Science) is a compulsory pre-requisite for CS students, and MATH11121 (Mathematical Foundations & Analysis) or equivalent is compulsory for CM students. Aims: The primary objective of this course is to build a strong foundational understanding of mathematical logic. It also aims to demonstrate the practical applications of logic within modern computer science, specifically focusing on areas like hardware verification, finite domain constraint satisfaction, and the verification of concurrent systems. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon successful completion of this module, students will be able to: ILO 1: Understand the syntax and semantics of Linear Temporal Logic (LTL), evaluate LTL formulas across computation paths, and verify the equivalence of LTL formulas. ILO 2: Comprehend model-checking algorithms and apply them to verify state transition systems. ILO 3: Encode problems from various domains (such as finite domain constraint satisfaction) into propositional logic. ILO 4: Understand OBDDs as a highly efficient data structure for the compact representation of propositional formulas, and demonstrate the ability to construct and combine them. ILO 5: Understand propositional logic and apply satisfiability-checking algorithms to solve related logical problems. ILO 6: Understand Quantified Boolean Logic (QBF) and apply appropriate reasoning algorithms to evaluate QBF formulas. ILO 7: Formally specify finite-state transition systems using transition graphs and symbolic representations. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Lectures consist of 22 sessions in total (2 per week), which includes dedicated feedback sessions focused on practical exercises. Study hours are allocated as 24 hours for lectures, 9 hours for practical classes and workshops, 2 hours for the written exam, and 65 hours for independent study. Administration & Assessment: Employability skills gained include analytical skills, innovation and creativity, problem solving, and research. Assessment methods include a written exam (70% weighting) and a written assignment, including an essay (30% weighting). Feedback and course materials, including comprehensive solutions to exercises, will be provided via the dedicated course website. The teaching staff includes Konstantin Korovin as the Unit Coordinator.

title: Processor Microarchitecture
description: Processor Microarchitecture.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP22111, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 2, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This course aims to reinforce and extend digital hardware development skills introduced in the first year, providing a view of the digital designer's role in taking an idea and implementing it in silicon. It examines different architectures and computing paradigms, finishing with a discussion on future technologies that may replace silicon as a processor building block. The syllabus is divided into two halves, with the first half covering an introduction to design approaches and complexity management, Stump specification and ISA (RISC vs CISC), Verilog and testing including verification and testbenches, sequential systems design via RTL and finite state machines, processor implementation, and an introduction to CMOS logic gates. The second half explores specialized processing architectures like DSPs and SIMD, microarchitectural structures such as register files and arithmetic circuits, FPGA operations, hardware design examples, CAD tools for logic synthesis and routing, verification and testing methods like BIST, timing and clocking issues, and future technologies like quantum dots and graphene. Pre/co-requisites: COMP12111 (Fundamentals of Computer Engineering) is a compulsory pre-requisite. Aims: The unit builds upon COMP12111 concepts to expand students' understanding of modern computing systems operations. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will learn to describe CAD tool stack components, explain ISA design factors and RISC versus CISC differences, explain processor design stages using Verilog, apply design methodologies for complex digital systems, design sequential systems with datapath and control elements, implement RTL using Verilog, evaluate VLSI CAD tool chains, and analyze processor design choices regarding cost, performance, and emerging trends. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Weekly activities include one hour of asynchronous videos supported by Blackboard quizzes, a one-hour synchronous activity for lab support, and a two-hour synchronous laboratory session in weeks 3 through 11. Total study hours consist of 22 lecture hours, 18 hours of practical classes and workshops, a 2-hour written exam, and 58 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical skills, innovation, and problem-solving. Assessments include a written exam weighted at 50% and a practical skills assessment weighted at 50%. Feedback is provided via formative Blackboard quizzes, automated lab marking, and email. Recommended reading includes texts by Furber, Clements, and Thomas, and the unit coordinator is Paul Nutter.

title: Microcontrollers
description: Microcontrollers. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP22712, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 2, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This course unit serves as a strong practical reinforcement of the software/hardware interface. Key themes covered include efficient low-level programming in RISC-V assembly, controlling hardware devices through software, developing applications with both hardware and software components, processor support for hardware-software communication, system software design, low-level debugging and testing, and reading, understanding, and using hardware technical documentation. The module focuses heavily on practical laboratory work utilizing custom-designed development boards created in-house. Completing this unit provides a solid understanding of low-level software, hardware-software co-design, and embedded programming, equipping students to compete for specialized roles not typically open to standard programmers. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP15111 (Fundamentals of Computer Architecture) is a compulsory pre-requisite. Aims: The course aims to familiarize students with using microprocessors for simple control and interfacing applications, which includes building modular low-level software, separating user and operating system tasks, and hardware-software interfacing. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will learn to explain how von Neumann architectures work in detail and apply this understanding to develop efficient low-level code, customize and modify input/output hardware devices to build resource-efficient applications, and design and build appropriate software interfaces connecting I/O devices with an embedded controller. Furthermore, students will employ hardware interrupts to design applications that utilize hardware resources efficiently and design and develop well-structured, large-scale embedded programs. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: There are no formal lectures for this unit; instead, teaching is conducted through laboratories, comprising 44 hours in total across 22 two-hour sessions. Total study hours involve 48 hours of scheduled practical classes and workshops alongside 52 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical skills, innovation and creativity, problem-solving, and others. The module is assessed entirely through a practical skills assessment, weighted at 100%. Feedback methods include direct verbal feedback and advice during scheduled laboratory sessions, automatic feedback from tools indicating exercise success or failure, and written feedback provided by annotating listings from submitted exercises. Recommended reading materials include texts on ARM assembly language and system design by Hohl (2014), Gibson (2007), and Sloss (2004). The unit coordinator is Pavlos Petoumenos.

title: Database Systems
description: Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP23111, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 2, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit, though students must contact the unit lead before choosing. Overview and Syllabus: Databases are foundational components of modern computing architectures, with data management evolving from fragmented file systems to organization-wide strategies recognizing data as a vital asset. At the heart of this transformation are Database Management Systems (DBMSs), which serve as the essential software infrastructure for managing data reliably and efficiently. Modern DBMSs embody advances in algorithms, data structures, programming language theory, conceptual modelling, concurrency control, and distributed computing to support transactional systems and data analytics. This course explores the principles underpinning the design, implementation, and management of databases, focusing heavily on the relational model, SQL, and database normalization. It covers the integration of databases with web and front-end systems, providing practical experience in building database-driven applications. Advanced topics include transaction management, database security, and alternative paradigms like NoSQL systems. The syllabus includes introductions to data management, (E)ER modelling, functional dependencies and normalisation, basic and advanced SQL (including stored procedures and triggers), web programming, transaction processing, application integration, NoSQL design, NoSQL in CouchBase, and data mining and warehousing, with each topic typically involving one workshop and laboratory work. Aims: The course provides a comprehensive exploration of databases, blending theory with hands-on practice so students develop essential skills in designing robust databases, writing advanced queries, and leveraging database technologies for real-world applications. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will demonstrate the ability to discuss and apply foundational database design principles including conceptual modelling and design trade-offs, design and implement a front end using appropriate technologies to execute CRUD operations, and explain basic database security mechanisms alongside classical threats and countermeasures. Additionally, students will explore the characteristics and use cases of NoSQL databases, execute complex SQL and NoSQL queries involving joins and aggregations, and analyze the relational model to derive and normalize schemas. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Teaching methods consist of 10 workshops in total, 3 lectures, weekly drop-in laboratory sessions, various weekly videos, and formative quizzes alongside additional reading. Total study hours include 3 lecture hours, 22 hours of practical classes and workshops, 15 hours of work-based learning or coursework, a 2-hour written exam, and 58 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical skills, innovation and creativity, project management, and problem-solving. Assessments comprise a written exam weighted at 30% and a practical skills assessment weighted at 70%. Feedback is provided via workshops, drop-in labs, discussion boards, and formally through coursework. Recommended reading includes database texts by Connolly, de Haan, Elmasri, Garcia-Molina, Kifer, Morton, and Ramakrishnan. The teaching staff includes Thomas Carroll as the Unit Coordinator.

title: Introduction to AI
description: Introduction to AI.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP24011, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 2, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This unit constitutes an introduction to the field of Artificial Intelligence, providing a broad overview of the subject while serving as a foundation for more detailed third-year courses such as AI and Games, Cognitive Robotics, Natural Language Understanding, and Computer Vision. The syllabus is divided into six main topics: search and planning (including adversarial games and classical planning), logic and reasoning (covering first-order logic and default logic), AI and probability (including Bayes' networks and uncertainty representations), knowledge representation using formal ontologies, peripheral robotics concepts like sensor integration and simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM), and philosophical issues such as the Turing test and consciousness. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP13212 (Data Science) is a compulsory pre-requisite for all students, alongside COMP11120 for CS students and MATH11121 or equivalent for CM students. Aims: The course aims to equip students with the ability to implement basic search and planning algorithms, apply first-order logic to model physical situations, develop formal ontologies, manage models of uncertainty, solve sensor integration problems in robotics, and appreciate the central philosophical problems connected with AI. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon completion, students will be able to apply language models and information extraction techniques, apply first-order logic to reason about actions, develop formal ontologies, implement basic search and planning algorithms for real-world problems, execute SLAM in robotics, and select appropriate models of uncertainty in concrete problem-solving situations. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The module includes 2 hours of lectures per week (22 hours total) and 2 hours of lab sessions per fortnight (8 hours total). Total study hours consist of 22 lecture hours, 8 practical class hours, and 70 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Assessments include a written exam weighted at 80% and a written assignment weighted at 20%. Feedback is provided through the exam and coursework assessments, which include four specific labs focusing on games, constraints, SLAM, and BM25. Recommended reading features texts by Russell, Prince, Szeliski, and Ben-Ari. The unit coordinator is Ian Pratt-Hartmann.

title: Machine Learning
description: Machine Learning.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP24112, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 2, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This unit provides a general introduction to key concepts in machine learning and the development of the field. It covers essential knowledge on building supervised machine learning systems, including classification and regression, by exploring model architectures such as instance-based models, linear models, linear basis function models, kernel methods, single- and multi-layer perceptrons, and convolutional neural networks. Students will learn about loss functions like sum of squares error, regularization, and cross-entropy, alongside optimization approaches for training, including basic optimality conditions and stochastic gradient descent. The syllabus also provides fundamental knowledge on different types of machine learning models, such as parametric, non-parametric, deterministic, and probabilistic models, as well as essential knowledge on clustering analysis for grouping similar objects. Students will learn how to measure the performance of classification, regression, and clustering systems, and will explore machine learning phenomena like bias and variance issues, overfitting, and underfitting. The course imparts essential knowledge and practical skills for performing machine learning experiments, focusing on data usage for model training, selection, and testing. The teaching content is divided into ten chapters: Machine Learning Basics, k-Nearest Neighbours, Machine Learning Experiments, Typical Machine Learning Models, Loss Functions, Training and Optimisation, Artificial Neural Networks, Deep Learning and Convolutional Neural Networks, Unsupervised Learning, and Machine Learning Applications. The course contains a fair amount of mathematical and algorithmic content, is intended to be self-contained, and utilizes Python for lab work. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP11120 is a compulsory pre-requisite for CS students, and MATH11121 or its equivalent is compulsory for CM students. Aims: The unit aims to introduce the essential concepts behind key machine learning techniques, methodologies for building systems, algorithmic approaches for learning from data, and empirical methods for evaluating and optimizing performance. It covers both supervised and unsupervised learning paradigms, providing the necessary mathematical background to understand how these methods work for applications like computer vision, natural language processing, speech synthesis, medical diagnostics, intelligent robots, and vehicle autonomy. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon completion, students will be able to explain key clustering algorithms, identify and describe issues in machine learning, and describe fundamental concepts such as supervised and unsupervised learning, classification, and regression. Furthermore, students will be able to describe concepts in model training, validation, evaluation, and selection, thereby designing machine learning experiments and analyzing the results. They will also design and implement machine learning solutions to real-world problems and explain, compare, and select key supervised learning techniques based on their limitations, advantages, trade-offs, and commercial concerns. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Weekly lectures feature structured input, exploratory activities, brief presentations, tutorial practice, and question-answer sessions. Bi-weekly laboratories function as drop-in help desks where Graduate Teaching Assistants (GTAs) provide support for lab script problems focused on mathematical programming and the implementation of machine learning techniques. Total study hours include 22 lecture hours, 12 practical class and workshop hours, a 2-hour written exam, and 64 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical skills, project management, problem-solving, and written communication. Assessments consist of a written exam weighted at 80% and a practical skills assessment weighted at 20%. Feedback is provided at the cohort level after marking, with individual feedback available from GTAs upon request. Recommended reading includes texts by Bishop, Alpaydin, Murphy, and Mitchell, and the unit coordinator is Tingting Mu.

title: Knowledge Based AI
description: Knowledge Based AI.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP24412, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 2, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: Intelligent systems need to be able to represent and reason about the world, and this course provides an introduction to the key ideas in knowledge representation and different types of automated reasoning. The course is a mixture of theoretical and practical work, ensuring that by the end of the module, students will know the principles these systems use and have experience implementing them in running systems. The syllabus is divided into four main areas: First-Order Logic and Automated Reasoning (covering syntax, semantics, translation to clausal form, ordered resolution, saturation-based proof search, and model construction); Prolog (covering syntax and execution, simple logical programs, relation to backward chaining with Horn clauses, and theorem proving); Knowledge Representation (exploring ontological engineering, categories and objects, events, semantic networks, description logics, and reasoning with default information); and Inductive and Abductive Reasoning (covering learning as a logic problem, learning with knowledge, Inductive Logic Programming, explanations and diagnosis, and explanation-based learning). Pre/Co-requisites: COMP24011 (Introduction to AI) is a compulsory co-requisite for this course. Aims: The aim of this course is to provide the conceptual and practical systems-building foundations for knowledge representation and reasoning in Artificial Intelligence. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon completion, students will be able to describe, differentiate, and apply different knowledge representation formalisms for modelling knowledge bases, and describe the syntax and semantics of first-order logic, including Datalog and Prolog fragments, to model problems. Students will also demonstrate forward and backward chaining reasoning methods, comparing their practical characteristics like efficiency and termination, and apply resolution-based reasoning techniques to establish properties of first-order problems. Furthermore, students will be able to explain the theoretical limitations of reasoning techniques, write Prolog programs to solve automated reasoning tasks, differentiate between deductive, inductive, and abductive reasoning for learning and inference, and relate knowledge-based approaches to real-world applications such as program synthesis or circuit design verification. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Teaching methods include 11 hours of lecture video material, 11 weekly synchronous sessions, and 10 hours of laboratories divided into five 2-hour sessions. Total study hours consist of 22 lecture hours, 10 hours of practical classes and workshops, a 2-hour written exam, and 77 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical skills and problem-solving. Assessments include a written exam weighted at 30% and a practical skills assessment weighted at 70%. Feedback on the various lab exercises is provided through written comments on the work and orally by the marker during lab sessions. Recommended reading features texts by Russell and Norvig, Blackburn, Bos, and Striegnitz (Learn Prolog Now!), Brachman and Levesque, and Merritt. The unit coordinator is Joseph Razavi, and additional course materials can be found on the School of Computer Science website.

title: System Architecture
description: System Architecture.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP25212, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 2, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This course introduces the key ideas behind the design of modern computer processors and explains how advanced architectural techniques are used to improve performance and efficiency. It covers performance limitations in modern computing systems, memory latency and the role of caching including cache organization and operation, and instruction pipelining, processor throughput, and superscalar architectures. Students will learn to handle dependencies and decision points during execution by addressing pipeline limitations, data and control hazards, and techniques to improve pipeline performance. The syllabus also explores running multiple tasks simultaneously via multi-threading concepts, processor support for multi-threading, and simultaneous multi-threading, alongside the motivation and possible organizations for multi-core processors. Furthermore, it addresses sharing data safely between processing units via cache coherence and consistency in multi-core systems. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP15111 (Fundamentals of Computer Architecture) is a compulsory pre-requisite. Aims: The course explains how modern computer processors work and why they use techniques like caching, pipelining, and parallel processing to run faster and more efficiently, helping students understand hardware-software interactions to use computing systems more effectively. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will describe main architectural techniques used to improve performance, reliability, and flexibility, and compare the strengths and weaknesses of different processor designs and multi-core organizations. They will explain and analyze how caching, pipelining, superscalar, out-of-order, and multi-threading techniques affect performance, explain the Von Neumann bottleneck and design methods to mitigate its impact, identify suitable processor architectures for specific requirements, and apply this knowledge to reason about trade-offs regarding performance, reliability, and resource usage. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Teaching methods include lectures, coursework, online quizzes, and a potential guest lecture from industry, with continuous feedback maintained through active classroom participation. Total study hours consist of 22 lecture hours, 12 hours of practical classes and workshops, a 2-hour written exam, and 64 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Assessments include a written exam weighted at 70% and a practical skills assessment weighted at 30%. Feedback is given after assessment. Recommended reading features texts by Patterson (2020) and Hennessy (2025). The unit coordinator is Davide Bertozzi.

title: Introduction to Visual Computing
description: Introduction to Visual Computing.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP27112, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 2, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit; however, students not from the School of Computer Science must obtain permission from both Computer Science and their home School to enrol. Overview and Syllabus: The field of Visual Computing brings together Computer Graphics, concerning the synthesis of images from computer models, and Image Processing, which deals with the analysis and understanding of images by computers. The unit covers Image Transformations, including image representations, resolution, colour models, and point and geometrical transformations such as camera calibration. It also covers Image Enhancement techniques like local processes, convolution, image smoothing, edge enhancement, edge detection methods (Prewitt, Sobel, Canny, Marr-Hildreth), the Hough transform for line detection, thresholding, blob detection, and rank order filters. Furthermore, students learn Fundamentals such as 2D and 3D coordinate systems, vectors, matrices, and geometric transformations. Finally, the syllabus covers 3D Modelling and Illumination, exploring the programmable graphics pipeline, 3D graphics primitives, meshes, rasterisation, hidden surface removal, the camera model, local illumination, Gouraud and Phong shading, writing shaders with GLSL, and surface detailing like textures and bump mapping. Aims: The course aims to enhance students' knowledge of how three-dimensional environments are represented and created interactively, how to combine 2D and 3D representations, and how to use mathematical techniques to build useful visual applications, preparing students for fields like medical imaging and computer simulations. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon completion, students will be able to apply the mathematics of 3D transformations and viewing, design systems using programmable-pipeline WebGL, and design image processing pipelines. Students will describe the principles of interactive computer graphics, image processing, and the rendering pipeline, while implementing fundamental computer graphics algorithms using three.js and image processing algorithms using C/C++ and the OpenCV library. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The module includes lectures spread over 12 weeks and 10 hours of laboratories focused on three.js exercises and C programming with OpenCV. Total study hours consist of 20 lecture hours, 30 hours of practical classes and workshops, 10 demonstration hours, and 40 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical skills, innovation, project management, and problem-solving. Assessments include a written exam weighted at 70% and a practical skills assessment weighted at 30%. Feedback is provided face-to-face and via marking in programming laboratories. Recommended reading features texts by Angel and Morris. The unit coordinator is Terence Morley.

title: Distributed Systems
description: Distributed Systems.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP28112, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 2, Teaching Period Semester 2. Available as a free choice unit, though students not from the Department of Computer Science must obtain permission from both Computer Science and their home School to enrol. Overview and Syllabus: This course assumes students possess a solid understanding of single-machine computing principles, machine communication, networking, and foundational notions of distributed computing. The syllabus covers the fundamentals of distributed computing, its real-world applications, and the core issues considered when designing distributed systems. Key topics include an introduction to the characteristics, challenges, and architectural models of distributed systems; remote invocation and distributed objects utilizing Java RMI, CORBA, and Web Services; and message-oriented middleware exploring synchronous versus asynchronous messaging, point-to-point messaging, and publish-subscribe models. Furthermore, the course explores concurrency, coordination, and distributed transactions—specifically the ordering of events, the two-phase commit protocol, and consensus algorithms—alongside caching, replication, security, and Service-Oriented Architectures (SOA) including REST and Web Services. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP16412 (Introduction to Programming 2) and COMP15212 (Operating Systems) are compulsory pre-requisites. Aims: The module aims to provide students with an awareness of the impact and benefits of distributed computing in daily interactions with modern technology, equipping them with essential knowledge, techniques, and basic skills required for advanced exploration in the field. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon completion, students will have acquired knowledge and understanding of the main principles, techniques, and methods supporting distributed systems functionality, and developed the ability to identify distributed computing applications and their societal impact. Additionally, students will understand the main issues when designing distributed systems, comprehend how these principles are applied in existing systems, and develop the ability to design and implement prototypical distributed computing applications using available technologies. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The course is delivered via lectures and workshops in the classroom, alongside laboratory sessions managed via Blackboard, individual self-study, and an online exam. Total study hours include 24 lecture hours, 10 hours of practical classes and workshops, a 2-hour online written exam, and 64 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical skills, innovation and creativity, and problem-solving. Assessments consist of a written exam weighted at 50% and a practical skills assessment weighted at 50%, with feedback provided three weeks following the submission of each piece of work. The unit coordinator is Sandra Sampaio, and the reading list and additional teaching materials can be found on the School of Computer Science website.

title: Exploring Enterprise
description: Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code MCEL10001, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 1, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by Alliance Manchester Business School and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This unit aims to develop students' problem-solving skills, creativity, and commercial awareness by acting as consultants to identify and evaluate business opportunities. It bridges the gap between academic knowledge and real-world applications, culminating in a consultancy project where students analyze a business and recommend innovative changes to its business model. The syllabus covers key steps for business model innovation, understanding enterprising individuals, internal company analysis including financial information interpretation, and external business eco-system analysis using tools like PESTLE and Porter's Five Forces. It also delves into idea generation and evaluation using SWOT, Ansoff's Matrix, and the Business Model Canvas, before concluding with strategies for turning ideas into action, risk management, and securing buy-in. Aims: The course equips students with the skills and mindset of effective CEOs, founders, and entrepreneurs. It trains students to master key business concepts and analytical tools, bridging academic theory with commercial reality, and promotes holistic value creation encompassing commercial, social, environmental, and well-being aspects. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will learn to apply structured analytical frameworks to generate actionable recommendations for business model innovation that create stakeholder value. They will design data-collection methodologies, critically evaluate secondary data, apply software tools to structure information into professional reports or presentations, and demonstrate commercial awareness combined with ethical, social, and environmental appreciation. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The module is delivered through 10 hours of lectures introducing the syllabus with built-in activities, alongside 10 hours of tutorials functioning as workshops and surgeries for project advice. Independent study accounts for 80 hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessments feature a formative 10-minute group presentation and a summative individual project weighted at 100%, taking the form of a 1500-word consultancy report or a 20-slide PowerPoint deck. Feedback is provided written via Canvas within 15 working days of submission. Recommended reading includes texts by Mullins, Worthington and Britton, and Atrill and McLaney, with Martin Henery serving as the Unit Coordinator. The unit is also offered as an option on the BA in Creative and Cultural Industries programme.

title: Entrepreneurial Skills
description: Entrepreneurial Skills.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code MCEL10002, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 1, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by Alliance Manchester Business School and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This unit is specifically designed to help students understand the processes entrepreneurs go through when researching and developing a business opportunity for presentation to potential investors. Students are expected to identify and develop an idea with commercialization potential using practical and creative techniques, researching key information such as market potential, strategy, and monetization capacity to support a Business Plan. The syllabus explores creativity and innovation, product and service design, value proposition design, and business model generation. It additionally covers business planning, marketing and market research, intellectual property, operations and resourcing for new ventures, pricing and financial strategies, and finding sources of finance. Aims: By the end of the module, students will have acquired fundamental skills and understanding relating to the entrepreneurial process of generating ideas with commercial potential, creating new ventures, and working intrapreneurially to support existing organizations through the development of products or services. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The unit is delivered through a series of formal lectures to introduce broad-level theoretical concepts, alongside workshops that evaluate case studies, utilize specific tools, and feature guest speakers who are active entrepreneurs. The module is fully supported via Canvas, where all lecture materials are available. Total study hours consist of 24 lecture hours and 76 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Assessments include a formative short proposal of the chosen business idea presented as a PowerPoint slide deck (maximum 500 words) and a summative written Business Plan for the proposed new venture (maximum 2000 words). Formative feedback is provided through lecture participation, direct replies to individual questions, email responses, and Canvas announcements, while summative feedback on the Business Plan is returned via Canvas. Recommended reading features texts by Stokes and Wilson, Osterwalder and Pigneur (including Value Proposition Design and Business Model Generation), Stutely, and Harris. The unit coordinators are Adam Frost and Robert Martin.

title: Exploring Enterprise
description: Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code MCEL10022, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 1, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by Alliance Manchester Business School and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This unit aims to develop students' problem-solving skills, creativity, and commercial awareness by acting as consultants to identify and evaluate business opportunities. It bridges the gap between academic knowledge and real-world applications, culminating in a consultancy project where students analyze a business and recommend innovative changes to its business model. The syllabus covers key steps for business model innovation, understanding enterprising individuals, internal company analysis including financial information interpretation, and external business eco-system analysis using tools like PESTLE and Porter's Five Forces. It also delves into idea generation and evaluation using SWOT, Ansoff's Matrix, and the Business Model Canvas, before concluding with strategies for turning ideas into action, risk management, and securing buy-in. Aims: The course equips students with the skills and mindset of effective CEOs, founders, and entrepreneurs. It trains students to master key business concepts and analytical tools, bridging academic theory with commercial reality, and promotes holistic value creation encompassing commercial, social, environmental, health, and well-being aspects. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will learn to apply structured analytical frameworks to generate actionable recommendations for business model innovation that create stakeholder value. They will design data-collection methodologies, critically evaluate secondary data, ethically apply relevant software tools (including AI) to structure information into professional reports or presentations, and demonstrate commercial awareness combined with ethical, social, and environmental appreciation through evidence-based problem-solving. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The module is delivered through 10 hours of lectures introducing the syllabus with built-in activities, alongside 10 hours of tutorials functioning as workshops and surgeries for project advice. Independent study accounts for 80 hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessments feature a formative 10-minute group presentation and a summative individual project weighted at 100%, taking the form of a 1500-word consultancy report or a 20-slide PowerPoint deck. Feedback is provided written via Canvas within 15 working days of submission. Recommended reading includes texts by Mullins, Worthington and Britton, and Atrill and McLaney. The unit coordinator is Martin Henery, and the module is also offered as an option on the BA in Creative and Cultural Industries programme.

title: Giving Meaning to Programs
description: Giving Meaning to Programs. [Image representing mathematical logic and functional programming abstractions] Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP31311, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 1. Available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: While programming language abstractions like functions are intended to allow high-level reasoning, they do not always behave intuitively; this unit develops a mathematical theory of program meanings that encompasses the counter-intuitive behavior of computations while preserving the ability to reason abstractly. The syllabus introduces fundamental technical tools through the study of Untyped Lambda Calculus, Simply Typed Lambda Calculus, and the proof method of logical relations. It explores the observational equivalence of programs, the function model of the simply typed lambda calculus, and PCF—the core of modern functional languages—alongside its denotational semantics. The course concludes with a taster of advanced topics such as polymorphism or concurrency. Pre/Co-requisites: Compulsory prerequisites include MATH10111 (Foundations of Pure Mathematics B), COMP11120 (Mathematical Techniques for Computer Science), or MATH11121 (Mathematical Foundations & Analysis). Aims: The unit is based on the principle that programs can be assigned a formal meaning, enabling students to reason about them rigorously in an abstract manner using standard technical tools. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon successful completion, students will be able to describe and analyze the behavior of terms in various models of computation, compute the denotations of programs and types, and prove results about programs using structural induction. Additionally, students will apply fundamental theoretical results about programming languages to reason about programs and prove program equivalence using appropriate models and techniques. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The unit follows a blended learning approach, utilizing detailed lecture notes, instructional videos, and workshops where learners explore key ideas through introductory activities and plenary discussions. Students are supported by staff during workshops and receive feedback on formative exercises via released solutions. Total study hours include 22 hours of practical classes and workshops and 78 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical skills, innovation, creativity, and problem-solving. Assessment consists of a written exam weighted at 80% and a practical skills assessment (take-home tests) weighted at 20%. Feedback is delivered through self-assessment quizzes, solutions for unassessed exercises, weekly study sessions, and direct feedback on two pieces of coursework. The unit coordinator is Andrea Schalk.

title: Implementing System-on-Chip Designs
description: Implementing System-on-Chip Designs.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP32211, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This course is structured around a central practical project supported by lectures that provide the necessary techniques and context for system-level design. The practical component involves migrating a moderately complex finite-state machine (FSM) into Verilog, integrating it with other System-on-Chip (SoC) components, and verifying its operation using a graphics drawing example that results in a visual display. The lecture series covers the scale and complexity of modern VLSI systems, advanced Verilog HDL features, functional simulation for constructing test harnesses, and systematic debugging. It also explores design and tool flows from source code to silicon, timing simulation, clocking strategies across different domains, essential CMOS technology concepts, and the physical constraints of layout design. The syllabus concludes with techniques for testing and validation and an analysis of future trends in silicon fabrication. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP22111 (Processor Microarchitecture) is a compulsory pre-requisite. Aims: The module provides a comprehensive overview of the processes involved in taking a concept through to a product-level chip, emphasizing the impact of design choices on performance, power, area, and feasibility through hands-on experience with the complete design flow. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon successful completion, students will be able to plan and execute digital hardware verification using simulation-based strategies, utilize industrial ASIC design flow terminology, and design FSMs at the Register Transfer Level (RTL). Additionally, students will interpret technical specifications, apply digital hardware synthesis flows using Verilog and industrial CAD tools, and analyze the theory and common pitfalls at each stage of the synthesis process. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The unit includes 11 lectures and 12 hours of laboratories (comprising one timetabled hour and one independent hour per week). Total study hours consist of 12 lecture hours, 12 hours of practical classes and workshops, a 2-hour written exam, and 74 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical skills, innovation, creativity, and problem-solving. Assessment is weighted equally between a written exam (50%) and a practical skills assessment (50%). Feedback is delivered orally during laboratory sessions, through annotated listings and diagrams, and via visual results from the constructed video systems. The unit coordinator is Davide Bertozzi.

title: The Internet of Things: Architectures and Applications
description: The Internet of Things: Architectures and Applications. [Image representing a connected ecosystem of sensors, actuators, and smart devices] Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP32412, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; while not traditionally available as a free choice unit, students from outside the School of Computer Science may enrol with permission from both their home School and the Department of Computer Science. Overview and Syllabus: This course introduces the paradigm shift of the Internet of Things (IoT), where objects designed for specific tasks are augmented with internet connectivity to create complex systems aimed at improving quality of life. The curriculum emphasizes the integration of data analytics with fundamental hardware components like sensors and actuators, focusing on the versatility of edge-nodes and the diversity of data types they produce. The syllabus covers the evolution of IoT, the organization of primary components including backend modules, gateways, and the IoT edge, and the construction of reference architectures based on specific design principles and real-world constraints. Key design issues explored include interoperability, reliability, hardware security for edge devices, and communication protocol stacks. Additionally, the course analyzes security, trust, and privacy—specifically identity management for edge devices—and presents detailed case studies in smart grids, home automation, and Industrial IoT (IIoT). Pre/Co-requisites: COMP28112 (Distributed Systems) is a compulsory pre-requisite. Aims: The unit aims to demystify the IoT concept by offering insight into architectural design and the interplay of diverse engineering and computer science fields within the ecosystem. It focuses on the 'Things' at the edge of the network, demonstrating how various requirements and constraints shape practical architectures across different industry segments. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon successful completion, students will be able to identify the basic organization and components of IoT systems, construct reference architectures step-by-step, and apply a comprehensive design methodology from initial specification to final deployment. Students will also evaluate the characteristics of wireless radio channels, define modulation techniques used in wireless communication networks, and identify security threats and mitigation techniques across all levels of the IoT stack. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Instruction is delivered through 20 lectures (2 hours per week) and two pieces of coursework. Total study hours consist of 20 lecture hours, 6 hours of practical classes and workshops, and 74 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical thinking, innovation, project management, problem-solving, research, and written communication. Assessment is conducted via a written exam weighted at 100%. Feedback is provided through weekly office hours and interactive Q&A sessions. The unit coordinator is Guodong Zhao.

title: Agile Software Pipelines
description: Agile Software Pipelines. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP33312, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; this module is not available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: Agile software engineering is now established as the industry standard for reliable and cost-effective development. This approach emphasizes the early and frequent delivery of releasable software artifacts to clients, utilizing disciplined but flexible project tracking and the continuous collection of active feedback to create high-quality, adaptable code bases. In agile projects, usable executable artifacts are created early in the lifecycle and delivered incrementally, often blurring the distinction between development and operational phases. While human management processes are iterative, the resulting software artifacts move through linear technical pipelines toward deployment. This course examines how the combination of iterative human processes and technical delivery pipelines provides real value and return on investment. Specific topics covered include an introduction to agile methodologies, requirements management, project planning and tracking, software quality management, DevOps practices in an agile context, and retrospectives for process improvement. Pre/Co-requisites: Compulsory pre-requisites include COMP23311 (Software Engineering 1) and COMP23412 (Software Engineering 2). Aims: The unit provides a working understanding of software pipelines and their role in the development, delivery, and operation of systems, alongside the iterative human processes used to manage them. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon successful completion, students will be able to select and apply agile practices for planning and tracking progress to maximize client value, and explain or design simple software delivery pipelines following core DevOps practices. Students will also apply feedback-oriented practices to identify and correct problematic team or software behaviors and compare agile methodologies with traditional 'big up-front' approaches. Furthermore, students will learn to apply agile requirements elicitation techniques and a variety of software quality management practices, emphasizing the critical role of testing pipelines in managing project complexity. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The course utilizes workshops for discussion and practice, face-to-face and online interactive group activities (including industrial coaching games), and online reading materials, case studies, and videos. Students also engage in self-paced, TA-supported working exercises. Total study hours consist of 22 practical and workshop hours, a 2-hour written exam, and 76 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: The module is assessed via a 2-hour written exam weighted at 100%, supported by formative self-tests and group activities (each 2.5 hours in length). Feedback is provided through post-exam sessions, demand-based automatic feedback via Blackboard, and staff feedback during group activities. Recommended reading includes works by Shore, Beck, Cohn, Freeman, Koskela, Patton, and texts on Lean software development and real-world agile integration. The unit coordinator is Thomas Carroll.

title: User Experience
description: Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP33511, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; this module is not available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This unit provides a practical, high-level overview of the tools, techniques, and mindset necessary to competently approach a first job in user testing or user experience, serving as a basis for future self-study. The curriculum distinguishes UX from usability, noting that while usability often focuses on the technical engineering of interfaces, UX covers a wider remit of psychological aspects of user behavior, defined by ISO 9241-210 as a person's perceptions and responses resulting from the use or anticipated use of a product. Key topics include UX concepts, history, and landscape; visual design; practical ethics; gathering and modeling user requirements; prototyping and rapid application development; accessibility; usability; emotion and engagement; user evaluation; human-in-the-loop systems; digital phenotyping; and evaluation analysis. The course operates as a 'flipped classroom,' where students are expected to engage with notes and videos before attending seminars for discussion. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP23311 (Software Engineering 1) and COMP23412 (Software Engineering 2) or demonstrable equivalents are compulsory pre-requisites. Aims: The module aims to equip students with the knowledge to communicate professionally regarding UX work, make sensible suggestions based on a complex understanding of the domain's scientific principles, and develop a consultancy mindset aware of various stakeholder interests. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon successful completion, students will be able to assess UX study scenarios, design methodologies for requirement gathering and evaluation, and select appropriate methods for analyzing designs. They will elaborate on topics like accessibility, usability, and emotion, explain the relationship between UX and software development principles, and conduct independent research to support a formulated opinion on key UX topics. Additionally, students will define UX terminology, evaluate concepts related to the scientific method in UX, and explain the importance of standards, technologies, and guidelines in the UX process. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Teaching is conducted through twenty-one sessions, including one lecturer-led and one student-led seminar per week, plus an extra session for revision. Total study hours consist of 21 seminar hours, 38 hours of practical classes and workshops, a 1-hour written exam, and 40 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills developed include analytical skills, innovation, problem-solving, research, and written communication. Assessment includes an open-book human-marked exam (50%), a computer-marked assessment (20%), and two written assignments (15% each). Feedback is provided through face-to-face communication and written comments on coursework. Recommended reading features 'Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance' by Robert M. Pirsig. The unit coordinator is Simon Harper.

title: AI and Games
description: AI and Games.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP34111, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; this module is not available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This module explores the foundational and practical aspects of Artificial Intelligence within the context of games. The syllabus covers the definition of games and pay-off functions, representations in normal and extensive forms, and strategies for decision-making. It investigates what it means to play a game well, focusing on best-response strategies, Nash equilibrium properties, and algorithmic methods for finding good game plans such as the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning. Students also learn how to develop game plans through exploration and exploitation, self-play, Monte Carlo Tree Search, and systems like AlphaGo. Pre/Co-requisites: Compulsory pre-requisites include COMP11120 (Mathematical Techniques for Computer Science), COMP13212 (Data Science), MATH11121 (Mathematical Foundations & Analysis), and MATH10111 (Foundations of Pure Mathematics B). Aims: The course introduces students to non-cooperative game theory and the Nash equilibrium solution concept, developing different approaches to effective play. During the first six weeks, theoretical material is developed, followed by five weeks of practical work where students develop an AI agent for a specific game. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Upon completion, students will be able to analyze what it means to play a game well by formulating best-response strategies, define core game components and distinguish between normal and extensive representations, and assess learning-based approaches like self-play. They will also apply and compare algorithmic methods for equilibrium computation and define strategies as complete decision-making plans. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Teaching involves 12 hours of lectures, 10 hours of practical classes and workshops, 3 demonstration hours, and 5 project supervision hours. Total study hours consist of 30 contact hours and 70 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Assessment is weighted equally between a written exam (50%) and a written assignment (50%). The unit coordinator is Jonathan Shapiro.

title: Cognitive Robotics
description: Cognitive Robotics.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP34212, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. This module is available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This unit provides an in-depth understanding of cognitive robotics, analyzing the selection and combination of methods from robotics, AI, psychology, and neuroscience to design intelligent behavior in interactive robots. The syllabus includes topics such as robot technologies, sensors, actuators, machine learning for robotics, developmental and neuro-robotics, evolutionary and swarm robotics, social robotics, and human-robot interaction. It also covers language learning, speech interfaces, robot tutors, and ethics. Practical labs focus on using machine learning, such as deep learning, for robot vision and language. Aims: The unit aims to provide an in-depth understanding of how psychological and neuroscience perspectives on behavior and intelligence inform the design of robotics models. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will be able to analyze methods and technologies for robotics research, understand how psychology and neuroscience inform intelligent design, and compare and apply machine learning methods for robot behavior. They will also discuss the state of the art in intelligent robotics and the role of ethics in responsible innovation. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The course consists of 16 lectures and 8 practical class/workshop hours. Total study hours include 16 lecture hours, 8 lab hours, and 76 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Employability skills gained include analytical skills, innovation, problem-solving, research, and written communication. Assessment is comprised of a written exam (70%) and a written assignment including an essay (30%). Feedback is provided on reports and orally during office hours and labs. Recommended reading includes 'Cognitive Robotics' by Cangelosi & Asada. The unit coordinator is Angelo Cangelosi.

title: Mathematical Topics in Machine Learning
description: Mathematical Topics in Machine Learning.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP34312, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. This module is available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This course covers advanced theoretical topics such as empirical risk minimization, regularization, bias/variance theory, and the probabilistic view of likelihood versus loss. It explores information theory (KL-divergence, cross-entropy), optimization theory including gradient descent and convexity, and dimensionality reduction through matrix algebra (PCA, spectral embedding). The 11-week syllabus covers the foundations of supervised learning, probabilistic modeling, generalization failure, model combination/ensembling (Bagging), sources of risk, PAC learning, and Rademacher complexity as a measure of function class capacity. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP24112 (Machine Learning) is a compulsory pre-requisite. Aims: The course introduces the theoretical foundations of supervised machine learning for students who want to understand why models work through a rigorous mathematical treatment of probability, matrix algebra, and calculus. It prepares students to engage with modern research literature for research careers in industry or academia. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will be able to define supervised learning problems using probabilistic notation, describe Rademacher complexity for generalization bounds, and critically assess limitations of classical learning theory in overparameterized models. They will also evaluate how model combination affects generalization error, analyze gradient descent algorithms, and derive theoretical frameworks for generalization error like PAC bounds. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The unit consists of four major topics delivered in 2-week blocks, featuring videos and readings reinforced by interactive sessions and weekly MCQs. After Easter, students read and present classic research papers in groups. Total study hours include 11 lecture hours, 11 hours of practical classes and workshops, a 2-hour written exam, and 76 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Assessments include a written exam (80%) and other methods such as a poster presentation (20%). Feedback is provided via discussions of correct answers. Recommended reading includes texts by Kevin Murphy and Sidney Chan. The unit coordinator is Gavin Brown.

title: Computational Game Theory
description: Computational Game Theory.  Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP34612, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; this module is not available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This module explores the intersection of game theory, AI, and economics, addressing how game theory provides insights for multi-agent systems and computer networks while presenting challenges for learning and communication. The syllabus covers concepts and computational solutions for non-cooperative and cooperative game theory, machine learning techniques to address issues arising from game theory applications, and mechanism design (reverse game theory) for crafting game rules. A major group project running over five weeks requires students to apply these theories to solve a competitive pricing game. Pre/Co-requisites: Compulsory pre-requisites include COMP11120 (Mathematical Techniques), COMP13212 (Data Science), MATH11121 (Mathematical Foundations), and MATH10111 (Foundations of Pure Mathematics B); COMP34111 (AI and Games) is a desired co-requisite, and COMP24112 (Machine Learning) is desired. Aims: The course teaches fundamental concepts and computational methods to model agent interactions and build skills in optimization for game analysis. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will master cooperative game theory including coalition formation and Shapley value calculations, and analyze learning scenarios for games with imperfect information. They will formulate reaction function learning problems, master mechanism design to solve pricing and application rules, and assemble these skills to evaluate practical competitive pricing games. Additionally, students will master non-cooperative theory, including Nash and Stackelberg games, by formulating payoff functions and computing best strategies via derivative methods. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The unit includes 12 hours of lectures, 6 tutorial hours, 5 workshop hours, and 30 practical hours, alongside 47 hours of independent study. Administration and Assessment: Assessment is weighted equally between a written exam (50%) and a written assignment (50%). The unit coordinator is Xiaojun Zeng.

title: Natural Language Processing
description: Natural Language Processing. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP34711, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; this module is not available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This course covers the linguistic and algorithmic foundations of Natural Language Processing (NLP), exploring challenges in textual data processing, document semantics, and speech patterns. It considers rule-based, machine learning, and deep learning approaches, including the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) for tasks like text classification, disambiguation, and speech recognition. The syllabus specifically includes lexical semantics, language models, deep learning for NLP, speech synthesis, and ethical considerations. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP24112 (Machine Learning) is a compulsory pre-requisite, though materials are provided for those who have not taken it. Aims: The unit enables computers to process 'natural language' as a key area of AI, explaining the components and applications required for large-scale written and spoken data. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will implement and evaluate NLP tasks, explain deep learning techniques for NLP systems, and discuss challenges in real-world data processing. They will also learn how LLMs and semantic representations are built and assess the ethical and societal impacts of deploying NLP systems. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Teaching involves weekly workshops and lectures organized as a blend of presentations and group activities. Weekly laboratories serve as hands-on sessions for trying techniques and providing feedback on coursework. Total study hours include 22 lecture hours, 22 practical hours, a 2-hour exam, and 54 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment consists of a written exam (70%) and a written assignment (30%). Feedback is provided face-to-face and via Blackboard forums. Recommended reading includes texts by Bird, Kamath, and Jurafsky. The unit coordinator is Goran Nenadic.

title: Natural Language Understanding
description: Natural Language Understanding. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP34812, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; this module is not available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: Building on NLP foundations, this unit dives deeper into how machines recognize meaning. Students gain hands-on experience in solutions for natural language understanding (NLU), developing know-how for human-machine communication technologies. The syllabus covers symbolic parsing, logical representations, vector-based representations (contextualized embeddings), neural language models, sequence classification, machine reading comprehension, and sequence-to-sequence translation. It also addresses the weaknesses of state-of-the-art NLU. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP34711 (Natural Language Processing) is a compulsory pre-requisite. Aims: The unit aims to introduce computational methods for interpreting language and provide an overview of state-of-the-art solutions and real-world applications. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will discuss task formulations like sequence labeling and translation, differentiate between parsing algorithms for meaning representation, and compare approaches for entity recognition and sentiment analysis. They will relate NLU to question-answering and conversational agents and develop a solution for a real-world problem. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The course features weekly asynchronous lectures, synchronous workshops, and fortnightly labs. Total study hours include 20 lecture hours, 10 practical hours, and 70 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment includes a written exam (70%) and a practical skills assessment (30%). Feedback includes live coding in workshops and cohort-level exam feedback. The unit coordinator is Riza Batista-Navarro.

title: Chip Multiprocessors
description: Chip Multiprocessors. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP35112, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: As single-processor performance hits technological limits, the industry has pivoted to integrating multiple processors on a single chip to exploit parallelism. This course explores the resulting hardware and software challenges. The syllabus includes technology trends, ILP versus Thread-Level parallelism, SIMD and MIMD architectures, and the case for shared memory. Detailed topics include cache coherence (MESI protocol), directory-based coherence, programming with locks and barriers, OpenMP, MPI, and transactional memory. It also covers GPGPUs, CUDA, and data-driven parallelism. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP25212 (System Architecture) is a compulsory pre-requisite. Aims: The unit explores hardware and software issues arising from single-core speed stagnation and the move to parallelism. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will describe issues in CMP design (CPUs and GPUs), evaluate programming language extensions for shared memory, and compare hardware structures for synchronization and speculation. They will evaluate concurrency support (threads, locks, barriers) through parallel programming exercises. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The course consists of 24 lecture hours and 76 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment is comprised of a written exam (70%) and a written assignment (30%). Feedback is provided on lab reports and through previous exam questions. The unit coordinator is Pierre Olivier.

title: Algorithms and Complexity
description: Algorithms and Complexity. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP36111, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This unit provides a self-contained introduction to Computational Complexity Theory. The syllabus covers directed graphs (Kosaraju's algorithm), union-find, the Grzegorczyk hierarchy, flow networks (min-cost max-flow), and string-processing. It also delves into Turing Machines, computability, the Entscheidungsproblem, separation theorems, and SAT variants. Hardness and reductions are explored through Cook's theorem, graph problems (3-colouring, Hamiltonian circuits), the TSP, and integer linear programming, as well as Savitch's and Immerman-Szelepcsényi theorems. Pre/Co-requisites: Compulsory pre-requisites include COMP11120, COMP11212, and COMP26120; for CM students, MATH11121 or equivalent is required. Aims: The unit familiarizes students with the core concepts and techniques of complexity theory. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will analyze algorithms like matching and flow networks, recite the hierarchy of complexity classes in the Turing model, and determine the complexity of various problems. They will define reduction and computational hardness (especially NP-hardness) and prove central theorems like Cook's and Ladner's theorems. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Teaching involves two 1-hour lectures per week and coursework, with 22 total lecture hours and 78 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment includes a written exam (80%) and set exercises (20%). Feedback is provided through coursework in weeks 4, 7, and 10. Recommended reading includes Sipser and Arora. The unit coordinator is Ian Pratt-Hartmann.

title: Mathematical Systems and Computation
description: Mathematical Systems and Computation. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP36212, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science and available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: Mathematical systems model everything from physics to AI, but their computational solutions often rely on algorithms that approximate problems. The course explores finite precision computation, error propagation, numerical solutions for differential equations, and optimization algorithms to maximize performance. These are applied to engineering systems and neural networks. The unit develops skills for the hardware-software interface and hardware accelerators. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP11120 is a compulsory pre-requisite; C/C++ experience is desirable. Aims: The course investigates real-world problems described via mathematical systems and the impact of numerical computation on their performance. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will describe finite precision issues (floating point, mixed-precision) and explain techniques for improved accuracy like stochastic rounding. They will apply numerical algorithms for differential equations and linear algebra, alongside bio-inspired optimization algorithms like evolutionary search. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The module includes 24 lectures, independent study via online videos, and coursework implementing theories on real-world problems. Total study hours include 12 lecture hours and 88 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment is 100% written assignment. Feedback is continuous in class and individual for coursework reports. Recommended reading includes Muller and Suli. The unit coordinator is Oliver Rhodes.

title: Graphics & Virtual Environments
description: Graphics & Virtual Environments. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP37111, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; this module is not available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This course covers modern computer graphics from scene modeling to animation and rendering, emphasizing quality and performance. Part 1 (Modelling) includes Bezier curves, splines, NURBS, subdivision surfaces, and colour matching/spaces. Part 2 (Animation) covers keyframing, skinning, physics-based/procedural animation, and collision detection. Part 3 (Rendering) explores the rendering equation, ray tracing, path tracing, radiosity, and volume rendering. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP27112 (Introduction to Visual Computing) is a compulsory pre-requisite. Aims: The unit provides principles of graphics modeling, animation, and rendering. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will describe physics-based animation, compare rendering approximations by visual fidelity, and select combinations of modeling techniques for 3D problems. They will also create novel rendering solutions and describe the rendering equation as a mathematical representation of real-world illumination. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The course uses a flipped classroom model with videos and readings before workshops where students work in groups. Total study hours include 11 workshop hours, a 2-hour exam, and 87 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment includes a written exam (90%) and set exercises/quizzes (10%). The unit coordinator is Zahra Montazeri.

title: Computer Vision
description: Computer Vision. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP37212, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; this module is not available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This unit introduces tools for recognition (identifying things in images) and 3D interpretation (understanding dynamic scenes from 2D images). Applications include medical analysis, face recognition, robotics, and surveillance. The syllabus covers image interpretation mechanisms like thresholding, edge finding, mathematical morphology, Hough Transform, template matching, and Active Contour Models (Snakes). It also covers statistical models (Active Shape Models) and 3D depth recovery through stereopsis and motion tracking. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP11120 and COMP27112 are compulsory pre-requisites. Aims: The course provides a broad introduction to image interpretation and algorithmic tools, exploring the importance of modeling. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will describe image segmentation as a clustering problem, compare interest points and local features for scene reconstruction, and analyze model-based versus image-based recognition. They will also analyze stereopsis matching algorithms and apply rigid/non-rigid image registration to biomedical applications. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The course features 22 lectures, self-test questions, and optional practical exercises with MATLAB scripts. Total study hours include 23 lecture hours and 77 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment is weighted at 70% for the written exam and 30% for written assignments. Feedback is provided on six pieces of coursework. The unit coordinator is Aphrodite Galata.

title: Advanced Distributed Systems
description: Advanced Distributed Systems. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP38311, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 1. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; this module is not available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This course explores categories and paradigms of distributed systems, focusing on cloud computing concepts, economics, and resource management (provisioning, scheduling, load balancing, energy, and QoS). It also covers Big Data drivers (the 4Vs), batch processing (MapReduce), interactive systems (NoSQL), and streaming data. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP23111 (Database Systems) and COMP28112 (Distributed Systems) are compulsory pre-requisites. Aims: The unit provides insights into advanced distributed systems and cloud computing, specifically referencing data-intensive applications. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will describe categories of distributed systems, compare current paradigms for real-world applications, and explain resource management principles for Clouds. They will also identify technology drivers for big data and explain the trade-offs in big data platform design. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Teaching includes 11 hours of workshops, a 2-hour laboratory session, and weekly online quizzes. Independent study totals 89 hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment is 100% written exam. Formative feedback is provided through workshop activities and online quizzes.
