Recommended reading includes texts by Foster, Kleppmann, and Miner. The unit coordinator is Rizos Sakellariou.

title: Cybersecurity: Principles and Secure Software Systems
description: Cybersecurity: Principles and Secure Software Systems. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP38412, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered by the Department of Computer Science; this module is not available as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: This course focuses on software-intensive systems, including AI and cyber-physical systems (CPS), integrating classical security with automated and formal verification. The syllabus covers threat modeling (STRIDE), foundations of cryptography (symmetric/asymmetric encryption, RSA, hash functions, MACs), and software vulnerabilities (memory safety, injections, race conditions). It also covers secure coding, supply-chain security, and automated analysis via model checking and formal specification in languages like C, Rust, and Python. Pre/Co-requisites: COMP15111 and COMP11212 are compulsory pre-requisites. Aims: Students learn to reason about security as a 'system property' from design to formal verification, gaining practical experience with automated tools. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will apply cryptographic and authentication schemes, assess automated security tools, and identify common vulnerabilities to apply secure coding practices. They will evaluate modern system challenges (AI, CPS), apply formal verification to detect software vulnerabilities, and classify security threats. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The course involves 20 lecture hours, 20 hours of practical classes/coursework, and 60 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment consists of a written exam (70%) and a written assignment (30%). Feedback is delivered through in-class exercises. Recommended reading includes Stallings and the Handbook of Model Checking. The unit coordinator is Dominik Winterer.

title: Quantum Computing
description: Quantum Computing. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code COMP39112, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. Offered as a free choice unit. Overview and Syllabus: Quantum computing sits at the interface of computing, mathematics, and physics. The syllabus covers state transition systems (nondeterministic, stochastic, quantum), exponentiality, destructive interference, and measurement. It includes a review of linear algebra (inner product spaces, tensor products), pure quantum mechanics (unitary evolution, observables, no-cloning theorem), and entanglement (Schrödinger's cat, Bell inequalities). Detailed algorithms include Grover's (search), Shor's (factoring), Quantum Fourier Transform, and teleportation. Pre/Co-requisites: Students must be comfortable with advanced mathematics, specifically linear algebra. Aims: The course introduces students to a domain where certain problems classically deemed slow can be solved exponentially faster, such as factoring large numbers which would render RSA insecure. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will use linear algebra to express quantum concepts, define consequences of quantum scenarios, and interpret/analyze simple quantum circuits. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Teaching includes 20 lecture hours and interactive sessions as required. Total study hours involve 24 lecture hours and 76 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment is 100% written exam. Feedback is provided face-to-face or via email for formative exercise sheets. The unit coordinator is Richard Banach.

title: Independent Study and Technical Explanations
description: Independent Study and Technical Explanations. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code EEEN31001, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 1. This module is not available as a free choice unit and serves as an anti-requisite for UCIL units. Overview and Syllabus: Students independently study a technical topic related to their degree and create learning resources (videos, workbooks, interactive notes) to explain it to peers or the public. The syllabus covers finding and evaluating course material, creating graphics, recording videos, and giving feedback. Aims: The unit prepares students for industry by fostering independent learning, deep understanding of new topics, and communication skills. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will plan and record independent self-learning of emerging technologies, critically analyze information from research, and design learning material to communicate complex matters. They will also evaluate the effectiveness of technical learning materials. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The course involves 24 lecture hours and 76 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessments include a logbook/reflective journal (15%), peer feedback (15%), and a final learning resource (70%). Feedback is ongoing for logbooks and provided on the final submission. Recommended reading includes works by Clark, Mayer, Alley, and Tufte. The unit coordinator is James Brookes.

title: Enterprise Management for Computer Scientists
description: Enterprise Management for Computer Scientists. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code MCEL30031, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Variable. Available to Computer Science students ONLY and cannot be taken with other MCEL units. Overview and Syllabus: This unit enables students to analyze business management issues using professional tools and techniques. The syllabus explores strategy and market planning, covering the macro environment (PESTEL), industry analysis, competitor analysis, and marketing strategy (the 4 Ps). It also covers product development, business sustainability, and protecting intellectual property. Aims: The unit explores enterprises competing in a market and evaluates issues in bringing knowledge-based ideas to market. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will explain elements of business strategy using financial management tools, describe risk management, and resourcing plans. Skills include effective project planning, compiling financial statements (cash flow, profit/loss), assessing risks, and proposing workable funding plans. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: The course is delivered via lectures with case studies and informal group activities. Total study hours include 24 lecture hours and 76 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment is a 100% written exam. Formative feedback is provided through case study discussions and in-class tasks. The unit coordinator is Lee Webster.

title: Managing Finance in Enterprises for Computer Scientists
description: Managing Finance in Enterprises for Computer Scientists. Course Unit Fact File: Unit Code MCEL30032, Credit Rating 10, Unit Level 3, Teaching Period Semester 2. Available to Computer Science students ONLY and cannot be taken with other MCEL units. Overview and Syllabus: This unit investigates establishing and growing a business or new project, with emphasis on financial and risk management. Topics include adding value, financial statements (Cash Flow, P&L, Balance Sheet), diagnosing problems via break-even and ratio analysis, lean start-up, sources of funding (VC, Crowdfunding), and exit strategies. A consultancy session is included for assignment feedback. Aims: The module aims to develop skills for producing business plans for start-ups or organization projects while introducing finance tools and entrepreneurial thinking. Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs): Students will explain strategy elements, describe risk management, and identify resourcing issues. Skills include planning new ventures, manipulating financial statements, assessing risks, and using software to present professional business information. Teaching, Learning, and Study Breakdown: Methods include financial statement creation, case studies, and evaluation exercises. Total study hours consist of 24 lecture hours and 76 independent study hours. Administration and Assessment: Assessment is a 100% individual assignment due in week 11. Feedback is provided via consultancy meetings before submission and via Canvas for summative work. Recommended reading includes texts by Barrow and Atrill & McLaney. The unit coordinator is Robert Phillips.
